title: Espinacas con garbanzos
subtitle: Sevillian spinach with chickpeas, fried bread, and smoked paprika
excerpt: Wilted spinach folded through tinned chickpeas in a sauce thickened with fried bread, garlic, and cumin, finished with sherry vinegar and smoked paprika. A Sevillian tapa, deeply savoury and light enough to eat in quantity. Vegan as it stands.
sourceNotes: Espinacas con garbanzos is a signature dish of Seville, particularly associated with the tapas bars of the Triana and Santa Cruz districts. The dish reflects the Moorish culinary heritage of Andalusia; the combination of spinach, chickpeas, cumin, and fried bread as a thickening agent (a form of picada) is characteristic of medieval Andalusian cooking. It appears in regional cookbooks from the nineteenth century and is one of the standard tapas of every Sevillian bar. The dish was eaten as a Lenten preparation during the weeks before Semana Santa (Holy Week), when meat was avoided.

content.text: The fried bread paste transforms spinach and chickpeas from a simple sauté into a cohesive dish. Two or three slices of day-old bread, fried in olive oil with garlic until golden, then pounded or blended with a little cooking liquid, thicken the sauce and carry the cumin and paprika throughout. This technique is a simplified picada and appears across Andalusian and Catalan cooking.

Fresh spinach wilts quickly; add it in batches and let it collapse before adding the next. Frozen spinach works but must be squeezed thoroughly dry before use, or it will waterlog the sauce. The sherry vinegar goes in at the end; it is the acidity that lifts the whole dish.

items.name: Garlic
items.prepNote: 3 for the sauce, 2 for the bread paste

content.text: Heat half the {{olive-oil}} in a large frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{baguette}} slices and 2 cloves of the {{garlic}} and fry, turning, until the bread is golden on both sides, about 3 minutes. Remove to a blender or mortar. Add 4 tablespoons of water and blend or pound to a rough, thick paste. Season lightly.

In the same pan, heat the remaining {{olive-oil}} over medium heat. Add the remaining {{garlic}}, sliced, and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{paprika-smoked}} and {{cumin-ground}} and stir for 30 seconds. Add the {{spinach}} in two or three batches, letting each batch wilt before adding the next. Add the {{chickpeas-tinned}} and stir to combine. Stir in the bread paste and 100 ml of water. Cook over medium heat for 5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce thickens around the chickpeas. Add the {{sherry-vinegar}}, taste, and adjust seasoning. Serve warm with crusty bread.

items.fix: Squeeze excess water from the spinach before adding; add more bread paste or simmer uncovered for a few more minutes
items.cause: Spinach was too wet, or bread paste too thin
items.symptom: Sauce is watery and does not cling to the chickpeas

items.fix: Keep the heat at medium; the bread and garlic should turn golden, not dark brown
items.cause: Garlic or bread burned during frying

items.fix: Add the spices off the heat or at lower temperature and stir immediately; paprika scorches fast
items.cause: Added to very hot oil and overcooked

items.fix: The sherry vinegar at the end is not optional; taste and add more if needed

content.text: Espinacas con garbanzos is a classic Sevillian tapa, as tied to Seville as gambas al ajillo is to the rest of Andalusia. The cumin and chickpea pairing points to the Moorish roots in the region's cooking. Protein, iron, and greens in a single pan.
